Revenue from operations ₹6,541 crore, down from ₹6,729 crore — India and Africa growth wasn't enough to offset the North America decline this quarter.
Not separately disclosed in Q4 reporting reviewed — shown as — rather than estimated.
Collapsed from 22.8% a year ago — a genuinely severe compression, driven by the loss of high-margin US products (Lanreotide, generic Revlimid) plus a 20% YoY jump in R&D spending.

North America revenue fell to $155 million for the quarter, driven by a temporary Lanreotide manufacturing halt (a USFDA observation at a Greek contract manufacturer) and the expiry of generic Revlimid exclusivity — two of Cipla's most profitable US products losing earning power simultaneously.
Up from 6.3% a year ago (₹509 crore this quarter), driven by product filings and development for upcoming respiratory and peptide launches. Display-only.
Not disclosed in the Q4 FY26 materials reviewed — shown as — rather than estimated. Display-only.
Consolidated revenue ₹6,605 crore, an all-time high. Healthcare Services (hospitals) grew a somewhat slower 16%; Apollo HealthCo (pharmacy+digital) grew faster at 20%.

Consolidated EBITDA ₹1,017 crore. The core hospital segment margin actually moderated slightly (45bps YoY to 23.8%) — this quarter's overall margin improvement leans more on the pharmacy/digital turnaround than the hospital business getting structurally more profitable.
Up from 67% a year ago. Eastern-cluster hospitals ran highest at 77%; metro hospitals at 71%.
Average Revenue Per Occupied Bed, up 12% YoY — pricing/mix strength and occupancy improvement happening simultaneously, a healthy combination.
Not confirmed on a clean consolidated basis from Q4 disclosures reviewed — shown as — rather than estimated. The Healthcare Services segment alone was cited at 30.3% in an earlier quarter.
Network gross revenue ₹2,664 crore for the quarter. Full-year FY26 network revenue grew faster at 16% YoY to ₹10,538 crore.
Q4 operating EBITDA ₹682 crore, up 8% YoY. Full-year FY26 EBITDA margin was 26.2%, among the highest in the Indian hospital sector.
Held above 75% despite growing operational bed capacity — occupied bed days grew 8% YoY, meaning growth is coming from genuinely more patients, not just more beds sitting empty.
Among the highest in the Indian hospital sector — reflects Max's deliberately premium market positioning rather than a volume/scale strategy.
A strong capital-efficiency figure, supported by net debt/EBITDA held below 1x — genuine balance-sheet discipline alongside growth.
Consolidated revenue from operations ₹82,762 crore, the highest quarterly level in company history, driven by Infrastructure Projects, Energy Projects and Hi-Tech Manufacturing execution.
Down from 11.0% a year ago. Energy Projects saw the sharpest pressure (margin fell to 6.5% from 8.2%) on cost overruns and close-out costs in legacy hydrocarbon projects.
Record order book of ₹7,40,327 crore (+28% YoY) against FY26 revenue of ₹2,85,874 crore — roughly 2.6 years of already-secured future work, exceptional visibility for a project-execution business.

Recurring PAT (excluding one-off exceptional items) grew 18% YoY to ₹17,238 crore — the cleaner underlying figure. Reported Q4 PAT actually fell 3% YoY due to a swing in one-off items between periods.
Full-year revenue ₹27,480 crore. Q4 alone grew a slightly slower 11.6% YoY — revenue is structurally back-half loaded, with roughly 37% of the full year landing in Q4.
Down from 30.7% a year ago — employee costs and other expenses grew faster than revenue this quarter, a real cost-discipline question even as topline growth stays strong.
Order book of ₹73,882 crore against FY26 revenue of ₹27,480 crore — roughly 2.7 years of already-secured future work, backed by ~₹30,000 crore of FY26 order inflow.
Full-year PAT ₹6,048 crore. Q4 alone grew a slower 4.6% YoY — profit growth is lagging revenue growth slightly, a margin-dilution signal worth watching into FY27.

Revenue from operations ₹10,738 crore, exceeding the company's own FY26 guidance. Logistics revenue grew a much faster 55% YoY and Marine revenue 134% YoY — the diversification businesses are growing far faster than the core port business.
Q4 EBITDA ₹6,020 crore. International ports margin rose sharply to a record ~29% (from 13.7% a year ago) — the overseas portfolio is now genuinely accretive, not a growth-stage drag.
133.4 million tonnes handled this quarter. FY26 total cargo crossed 500 million tonnes for the first time by any Indian integrated transport operator — a genuine scale milestone.
Reflects continued heavy capex (₹15,320 crore in FY26) funding the ports-to-logistics transformation and international expansion, tied to an FY31 target of doubling revenue and EBITDA.
Q4 revenue from operations ₹32,439 crore. Full-year FY26 total income grew a much slower 3% YoY — the Q4 acceleration doesn't represent the full-year trend.

Q4 EBITDA ₹4,479 crore, up just 3% YoY even as revenue grew 20% — margin compression this quarter, reflecting the mix shift as newer, lower-margin capex-heavy businesses (like the copper plant) ramp up.

Full-year PAT ₹9,339 crore. This is overwhelmingly driven by a one-off ₹9,215 crore exceptional gain from selling stakes in Adani Wilmar and cement units to Ambuja Cements — full-year EBITDA was roughly flat YoY, a far less impressive underlying trend. Q4 alone swung to a net loss of ₹221 crore on higher depreciation from newly commissioned assets.

Management's own disclosed figure — a genuine, structural shift toward earnings stability as the incubation portfolio matures. Display-only: a higher figure generally signals more predictable earnings, though it also means less exposure to the newer, higher-growth-potential ventures.
Total income ₹89,513 crore. Q4 alone grew a slower 3.2% YoY, partly due to Middle East conflict-related capacity disruptions.
Available Seat Kilometres. Q4 capacity growth was a slower 3.4% YoY 'despite disruptions arising from the ongoing Middle East conflict,' per the company's own release.
Cost per Available Seat Kilometre excluding fuel rose to ₹3.00 — non-fuel costs are outpacing revenue growth, even as fuel costs (CASK ex-fuel... fuel CASK specifically) fell 11.5% YoY.

Profit excluding forex and exceptional items fell to ₹7,503 crore from ₹8,868 crore a year earlier — a genuine decline even on this 'clean' basis. Reported results were far worse: a full-year net loss of ₹2,394 crore, driven by sharp rupee depreciation and new labour-code costs.

Q4 consolidated total income growth was held down by mark-to-market losses on debt and equity portfolios (linked to Middle East conflict-driven market volatility). Excluding those MTM losses, the company says revenue growth would have been 14% — the underlying operating trend is stronger than the headline.

Q4 consolidated PAT ₹2,539 crore. Full-year FY26 PAT grew a similar 10% to ₹9,801 crore — solid but far below the group's historical 20%+ growth pace, reflecting a genuinely tougher year for the underlying financial-services businesses.
The lending arm (Bajaj Finance) remains the key growth engine — AUM grew 22% YoY to ₹5.09 lakh crore, with Q4 net total income up 21%. Lending is carrying the group while insurance disappoints.

A modest figure for a financial-services group, reflecting the holding-company structure (value is spread across subsidiaries) plus drag from loss-making emerging businesses (₹539 crore of losses in FY26).

Q4 revenue from operations nearly doubled to ₹1,018.51 crore. FY26 total income (ex-dividends) rose 78% to ₹3,274 crore — genuinely explosive top-line scaling off a small base, driven by lending, payments and insurance broking all ramping at once.

Consolidated Q4 PAT fell to ₹272.22 crore. The decline is a deliberate growth-vs-profitability trade-off: total expenses rose 327% YoY on heavy business-building investment, plus Jio Payments Bank's operating loss now consolidates fully into JFS's books (it became a 100% subsidiary in June 2025).
Jio Credit AUM surged to ₹25,711 crore, up 156% YoY (a ~149x jump since FY24 off a near-zero base), driven entirely by organic disbursements. Home loans/LAP are 45% of the book, corporate loans 44%.

Not meaningful yet — JFS carries a very large ₹1.33 lakh crore net worth (much of it from the Reliance demerger) against still-small early-stage profits, so a computed RoE would understate the business and mislead. Shown as — rather than a distorted figure.

metric_label: B2C Net Order Value Growth (Q4 YoY)
metric_note: B2C NOV reached ₹26,880 crore. This is the cleanest measure of real platform scale — Blinkit's NOV alone surged 95.4% YoY to ₹14,386 crore, now exceeding Zomato food delivery's NOV. Food delivery NOV grew a steadier 18.8%.

Like-for-like adjusted revenue growth of 64% is the honest figure. The headline reported revenue growth of 196% (to ₹17,292 crore) is mechanically inflated by Blinkit's marketplace-to-inventory (1P) accounting shift — reading the headline number badly overstates real growth.

Consolidated Adjusted EBITDA ₹429 crore. The single most important milestone this quarter: Blinkit turned Adjusted-EBITDA positive for the first time (₹37 crore) — the quick-commerce business is now demonstrably profitable at scale, not just growing.

Q4 PAT ₹174 crore, well above estimates. But full-year FY26 PAT actually fell to ₹366 crore (from ₹527 crore) — a heavier investment phase earlier in the year (especially Q1) means the strong Q4 doesn't represent the full-year trend. The quarterly trajectory (Q1 loss → ₹102cr Q3 → ₹174cr Q4) is the more relevant signal.

Consolidated revenue ₹5,028 crore. Driven almost entirely by store-count expansion (retail area +30% YoY) rather than existing-store productivity — revenue per square foot actually declined ~6% as like-for-like growth stayed in low single digits.

Expanded 242bps YoY, driven by gross-margin improvement (better mix, lower discounting) plus lower employee and rent cost per square foot. Note it fell sequentially from Q3's ~20.2% on seasonal softness.
Total retail footprint grew 30% YoY to ~17.7 million sq ft. Trent added 60 Westside and 212 Zudio stores in FY26 (~1.2 Zudio stores per day in Q4), with ~80% of new Zudio stores in Tier 2/3 cities.

Adjusted PAT ₹455 crore (standalone). Full-year FY26 PAT grew a more modest 13-25% depending on the measure and labour-code adjustment. Trent also announced its first-ever bonus issue (1:2) — a management confidence signal.
